title: Email notifications arriving hours late
body: Task assignment notifications used to arrive within a minute or two. For the past week, they've been arriving 2-4 hours late. This is causing people to miss deadlines because they don't know they've been assigned a task until hours later. It seems to be worse in the morning — notifications sent around 9-10am take the longest to arrive. Afternoon notifications are usually faster but still delayed.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for the detailed report — the time-of-day pattern is a useful clue.

To help me scope this: is this delay affecting just you, or have other team members reported the same thing? And to confirm — are other notification types (e.g., in-app notifications, Slack integrations if you use them) also delayed, or is it specifically email?

Also, do you know if anything changed about a week ago when this started — a new deployment, a change in team size, or a spike in task volume?

comments.author: reporter
comments.body:
It's not just me — a few other people on my team have mentioned it too. One of them started just checking the TaskFlow dashboard directly because they got tired of waiting. As far as I know it's specifically email notifications. I don't think we use Slack integration, and I haven't noticed any delay with the in-app stuff, though I don't always have the app open.

As for what changed... I'm not sure about deployments, but I think around that time we started getting these daily digest emails from TaskFlow? Like a marketing summary or something that shows up in the morning. I'm not sure if that's related or just a coincidence. Our team size hasn't changed.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
That daily digest email is a really promising lead — the timing lines up well with the morning delays you're seeing.

Could you (or one of your teammates) check the email headers on one of the delayed notification emails? Specifically, look for the `Date` header (when TaskFlow says it sent it) versus when it actually landed in your inbox. In Gmail you can do this via "Show original"; in Outlook it's "View message source." That will tell us whether TaskFlow is sending the email late or whether it's getting stuck somewhere in delivery.

This will help us figure out if the notification queue is getting backed up behind those digest emails.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: Hmm, I haven't done that before but let me try... OK so I just checked one from yesterday morning. The Date header says it was sent at like 9:15am but it didn't hit my inbox until around 11:45am. So it looks like there's a big gap between when TaskFlow queued it and when it actually arrived. That does seem like something is backing up on TaskFlow's end rather than our email server being slow, right? The afternoon ones I checked seemed closer — maybe 20-30 minutes delay instead of hours.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for checking those headers — that's very helpful and confirms the delay is on TaskFlow's side, not your email provider.

I have a working theory: those new daily digest emails are likely being sent in a large batch each morning, and they're clogging the same email pipeline that your task assignment notifications use. That would explain why morning notifications are delayed the most (they're stuck behind the digest batch) and why afternoon ones recover somewhat (the queue has drained by then).

One thing that would help confirm this: what time do those daily digest emails typically land in your inbox? If they arrive around 9-10am — right before the worst notification delays — that would strongly support this theory.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: Let me think... I usually see the digest email show up in my inbox sometime in the morning, yeah. I think it arrives around 9am or maybe a little after? It's one of the first things I see when I check email in the morning. So yeah, that does line up with when the delays are worst. That theory makes sense to me — if TaskFlow is blasting out a bunch of those digest emails at 9am it could be clogging things up for the regular notifications.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: [RESOLVED] {"title":"Email notifications delayed 2-4 hours due to daily digest emails saturating shared send queue","problem":"Task assignment email notifications that previously arrived within 1-2 minutes are now delayed 2-4 hours in the morning and 20-30 minutes in the afternoon. Multiple team members are affected, causing missed deadlines. The issue began approximately one week ago, coinciding with the introduction of a new daily digest email feature.","root_cause_hypothesis":"The new daily digest email feature sends a large batch of emails each morning (~9am) through the same email sending pipeline used by transactional task assignment notifications. This batch saturates the queue, causing transactional notifications to wait behind the digest backlog. The queue drains over the course of hours, which is why morning notifications are delayed the most (2-4 hours) and afternoon notifications see shorter delays (20-30 minutes).","reproduction_steps":["Assign a task to a user at approximately 9:00-9:15am (when daily digest emails are being sent)","Observe that the email notification does not arrive for 2-4 hours despite the Date header showing the correct send time","Assign a task to a user in the afternoon (after ~1-2pm)","Observe that the notification is still delayed but only by 20-30 minutes"],"environment":"Affects all users receiving email notifications. In-app notifications are unaffected. No Slack integration in use. No changes to team size or other configuration — only the introduction of daily digest emails correlates with the onset.","severity":"high","impact":"Multiple team members across at least one team are missing task assignment deadlines because they don't learn about assignments for hours. At least one user has abandoned email notifications entirely in favor of manually checking the dashboard.","recommended_fix":"Investigate the email sending pipeline for queue separation. The most likely fix is to either: (1) move digest emails to a separate, lower-priority queue so they don't block transactional notifications, (2) rate-limit or stagger the digest batch so it doesn't flood the queue all at once, or (3) use a dedicated sending pathway for time-sensitive transactional emails. Also check whether the email service provider has rate limits being hit by the digest volume.","proposed_test_case":"Send a batch of N digest-equivalent emails followed immediately by a transactional notification email. Verify the transactional email is delivered within the SLA (e.g., under 2 minutes) regardless of digest queue depth. This can be tested by measuring delivery latency of transactional emails sent concurrently with a simulated digest batch.","information_gaps":["Exact email service provider and queue architecture (internal implementation detail)","Size of the daily digest batch (how many recipients per morning)","Whether the email provider has rate limits being hit","Whether any error logs or queue depth metrics are available from the sending service"]}
